test.name: Narrative certainty must match a sub-Proved tier when research is not exhaustive
test.description: The richardson-parentage-not-exhaustive scenario has strong direct parentage evidence but research is NOT declared exhaustive (exhaustive_declaration.declared is false) and planned searches remain unexecuted, at least one of which could corroborate or contradict the parentage. The skill should select a tier no higher than `probable` AND write a narrative whose certainty matches that tier — it must NOT declare the case 'proved', claim 'all five GPS components are satisfied', or assert the research is 'reasonably exhaustive'. Captures a run that tiered `probable` but wrote Proved-level narrative language contradicting its own exhaustiveness state.

input.user_message: Write the proof conclusion for the parentage question.

judge_context: Should select a tier no higher than `probable`: research is not declared exhaustive (`exhaustive_declaration.declared` is false) and planned searches remain unexecuted, at least one of which could corroborate OR contradict the parentage — so `proved` is not available regardless of how strong the gathered evidence looks.

The narrative_markdown must NOT assert the conclusion is 'proved', that 'all five GPS components are satisfied', or that the research is 'reasonably exhaustive', while the recorded exhaustiveness state (declared: false) and the open plan items say otherwise. Narrative certainty must match the assigned tier — definitive proof language at a sub-Proved tier is the failure.
Should justify the chosen tier against the adjacent tiers, naming the specific outstanding searches that would be required to advance toward `proved` rather than presenting the search as complete.

narrative_markdown should read as a self-contained conclusion with inline citations, and use conditional phrasing consistent with `probable` (e.g. 'the preponderance of evidence indicates') rather than declarations of proof.
